headline: Illegal Money Lending Traps 380,000 Borrowers in Debt Spiral
context: PDRM estimated that 380,000 Malaysians owed money to Ah Long (illegal moneylenders) in 2025, with total underground lending estimated at RM8.4 billion charging rates of 120-360% annually, fuelled by rising cost of living and formal credit rejection.

cards.big: 380,000 Malaysians trapped by Ah Long — borrowing RM3,000 at 20% monthly means owing RM36,000 after one year
cards.sub: 8 deaths linked to loan shark harassment in 2025 — victims include a single mother in Ipoh and a retiree in Melaka

cards.big: Underground lending at RM8.4 billion exceeds legitimate micro-credit market — an entire shadow financial system operates openly
cards.sub: Ah Long operations generate RM2.8 billion in annual revenue — larger than many licensed financial institutions

cards.big: 72% of Ah Long victims are B40 workers who were rejected by banks and legitimate lenders first
cards.sub: Desperation pathway: bank rejection leads to licensed moneylender rejection leads to Ah Long — each step more predatory

cards.big: PDRM Ops Striking Force arrested 2,800 suspected Ah Long operators in 2025 but conviction rate was only 14%
cards.sub: Moneylenders Act penalties of RM250,000 fine rarely imposed — average fine was RM8,400 in 2025 prosecutions

cards.big: Ah Long thrive not because enforcement fails — but because the formal system rejected 380,000 people who still needed money
Should Malaysia create a government-backed emergency micro-loan facility at 4% interest as an alternative to illegal lending?
